question_text: Let A = {a, b, c} and the relation R be defined on A as follows: R = {(a, a), (b, c), (a, b)}. Then, write minimum number of ordered pairs to be added in R to make R reflexive and transitive.
question_html:
Let A = {a, b, c} and the relation R be defined on A as follows:
R = {(a, a), (b, c), (a, b)}.
Then, write minimum number of ordered pairs to be added in R to make R reflexive and transitive.
solution_text: Given relation, R = {(a, a), (b, c), (a, b)}. To make R reflexive we must add (b, b) and (c, c) to R. Also, to make R transitive we must add (a, c) to R. So, minimum number of ordered pairs to be added is 3.
solution_html:
Given relation, R = {(a, a), (b, c), (a, b)}.
To make R reflexive we must add (b, b) and (c, c) to R. Also, to make R transitive we must add (a, c) to R.
So, minimum number of ordered pairs to be added is 3.

question_html: Let f, g : R {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} R be defined by f(x) = 2x + 1 and g(x) = x2 - 2, {tex}\forall{/tex} x {tex}\in{/tex} R, respectively. Then, find gof.
solution_text: Given that, f(x) = 2x + 1 and g(x) = x 2 - 2, {tex}\forall{/tex} x {tex}\in{/tex} R Then (gof)x = g{f(x)}=g(2x+1)=(2x+1) 2 -2 = 4x 2 + 4x + 1 - 2 = 4x 2 + 4x - 1

= 4x2 + 4x + 1 - 2
= 4x2 + 4x - 1

solution_text: Given f(x) = 2x - 3 {tex}\forall{/tex} x {tex}\in{/tex} R Now, Let a, b {tex}\forall{/tex} R such that f(a) = f(b) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2a - 3 = 2b - 3 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = b {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f(x) Is One-One. Also, If x, y {tex}\in{/tex} R Such that f(x) = y {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x - 3 = y {tex}\forall \Rightarrow x = \frac{{y + 3}}{2} = g(y)\forall y \in R{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f(x) is onto and Therefore is bijective implies f(x) has an inverse Let f -1 Denote the inverse of f(x) Then {tex}{f^{ - 1}}(x) = g(x) = \frac{{x + 3}}{2}\forall x \in R{/tex}
solution_html:
Given f(x) = 2x - 3 {tex}\forall{/tex}x {tex}\in{/tex} R
Now, Let a, b {tex}\forall{/tex} R such that
f(a) = f(b)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2a - 3 = 2b - 3
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = b
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f(x) Is One-One.
Also, If x, y {tex}\in{/tex} R Such that
f(x) = y {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x - 3 = y
{tex}\forall \Rightarrow x = \frac{{y + 3}}{2} = g(y)\forall y \in R{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f(x) is onto and Therefore is bijective implies f(x) has an inverse
Let f-1 Denote the inverse of f(x) Then
{tex}{f^{ - 1}}(x) = g(x) = \frac{{x + 3}}{2}\forall x \in R{/tex}

question_text: Is the set of ordered pairs given at the end a function? If so, examine whether the mapping is injective or surjective. {(x, y): x is a person, y is the mother of x}.
question_html:
Is the set of ordered pairs given at the end a function? If so, examine whether the mapping is injective or surjective.
{(x, y): x is a person, y is the mother of x}.
solution_text: The relation is given by R={(x, y): x is a person, y is the mother of x}. Here, (x, y) is an ordered pair which associates each person (x) to his(her) mother (y). Now, each person will have only one mother. So we can say that for each value of x there is an association to a unique value of y. Hence,(x, y) represents a function. Also, more than one person may have the same mother. i.e., two or more distinct values of x may have the same value of y. So, (x, y) is many-one function. In other words,R is not injective. Finally, co-domain of R is the set of mothers of persons in domain, thus every element in co-domain is associated to at least one element of the domain. Therefore, R is surjective.
solution_html:
The relation is given by
R={(x, y): x is a person, y is the mother of x}.
Here, (x, y) is an ordered pair which associates each person (x) to his(her) mother (y).
Now, each person will have only one mother. So we can say that for each value of x there is an association to a unique value of y.
Hence,(x, y) represents a function.
Also, more than one person may have the same mother.
i.e., two or more distinct values of x may have the same value of y.
So, (x, y) is many-one function. In other words,R is not injective.
Finally, co-domain of R is the set of mothers of  persons in domain, thus every element in co-domain is associated to at least one element of the domain. Therefore, R is surjective.

solution_text: Given function, f(x) = cosx, {tex}\forall{/tex} x {tex}\in{/tex} R Now, {tex}f\left( {\frac{\pi }{2}} \right) = \cos \frac{\pi }{2} = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow f\left( {\frac{{ - \pi }}{2}} \right) = \cos \frac{\pi }{2} = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow f\left( {\frac{\pi }{2}} \right) = f\left( {\frac{{ - \pi }}{2}} \right){/tex} But {tex}\frac{\pi }{2} \ne \frac{{ - \pi }}{2} = 0{/tex} So, f (x) is not one-one Now, f(x) = cosx, {tex}\forall{/tex} x {tex}\in{/tex} R is not onto as there is no pre-image for any real number. Which does not belong to the intervals [-1, 1], the range of cos x.

Now, {tex}f\left( {\frac{\pi }{2}} \right) = \cos \frac{\pi }{2} = 0{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow f\left( {\frac{{ - \pi }}{2}} \right) = \cos \frac{\pi }{2} = 0{/tex}

Now, f(x) = cosx, {tex}\forall{/tex}x {tex}\in{/tex} R is not onto as there is no pre-image for any real number. Which does not belong to the intervals [-1, 1], the range of cos x.

question_text: If functions f : A {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} B and g : B {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} A satisfy gof = I A , then show that f is one-one and g is onto.
question_html: If functions f : A {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} B and g : B {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} A satisfy gof = IA, then show that f is one-one and g is onto.
solution_text: Given that, f : A {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} B and g : B {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} A satisfy gof = I A , One-one:Let x,y {tex}\in{/tex} A such that f(x)=f(y).Then, f(x)=f(y) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} g(f(x))=g(f(y)) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} gof(x)=gof(y) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I A (x) = I B (y) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = y Hence, f is one-one. Onto : Let x be an arbitrary element of A.Then, f : {tex}{/tex} A {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} B is a function so that f(x) {tex}\in{/tex} R. Let f(x)=y.Then, g(y)=g(f(x)) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} g(y)=gof(x) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} g(y) = I A (x) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} g(y)=x Thus, for every x {tex}\in{/tex} R there exists y = f(x) such that g(y) = x. So, g is onto.
solution_html:
Given that, f : A {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} B and g : B {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} A satisfy gof = IA,
One-one:Let x,y {tex}\in{/tex} A such that f(x)=f(y).Then,
f(x)=f(y)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} g(f(x))=g(f(y))
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} gof(x)=gof(y)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} IA(x) = IB(y)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = y
Hence, f is one-one.
Onto : Let x be an arbitrary element of A.Then, f : {tex}{/tex}A{tex}\rightarrow{/tex}B is a function so that f(x) {tex}\in{/tex} R.
Let f(x)=y.Then,
g(y)=g(f(x))
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} g(y)=gof(x)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} g(y) = IA(x)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} g(y)=x
Thus, for every x {tex}\in{/tex} R there exists y = f(x) such that g(y) = x.
So, g is onto.

question_text: Let f : R {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} R be the function defined by {tex}f(x) = \frac{1}{{2 - \cos x}},\;\forall x \in R{/tex} . Then, find the range of f.
question_html: Let f : R {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} R be the function defined by {tex}f(x) = \frac{1}{{2 - \cos x}},\;\forall x \in R{/tex}. Then, find the range of f.
solution_text: Given function, {tex}f(x) = \frac{1}{{2 - \cos x}},\;\forall x \in R{/tex} Let {tex}y = \frac{1}{{2 - \cos x}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2y - y cosx = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y cosx = 2y - 1 {tex} \Rightarrow \cos x = \frac{{2y - 1}}{y} = 2 - \frac{1}{y} \Rightarrow \cos x = 2 - \frac{1}{y}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow - 1 \leqslant \cos x \leqslant 1 \Rightarrow - 1 \leqslant 2 - \frac{1}{y} \leqslant 1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow - 3 \leqslant - \frac{1}{y} \leqslant - 1{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow 3 \geqslant \frac{1}{y}{\kern 1pt} \geqslant 1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{3} \leqslant y \leqslant 1{/tex} So, Range of y, that is f(x) is {tex}\left[ {\frac{1}{3},1} \right]{/tex}
solution_html:
Given function, {tex}f(x) = \frac{1}{{2 - \cos x}},\;\forall x \in R{/tex}
Let {tex}y = \frac{1}{{2 - \cos x}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2y - y cosx = 1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y cosx = 2y - 1
{tex} \Rightarrow \cos x = \frac{{2y - 1}}{y} = 2 - \frac{1}{y} \Rightarrow \cos x = 2 - \frac{1}{y}{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow - 1 \leqslant \cos x \leqslant 1 \Rightarrow - 1 \leqslant 2 - \frac{1}{y} \leqslant 1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow - 3 \leqslant - \frac{1}{y} \leqslant - 1{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow 3 \geqslant \frac{1}{y}{\kern 1pt} \geqslant 1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{3} \leqslant y \leqslant 1{/tex}
So, Range of y, that is f(x)  is {tex}\left[ {\frac{1}{3},1} \right]{/tex}

question_text: Let n be a fixed positive integer. Define a relation R in Z as follows {tex}\forall{/tex} a, b {tex}\in{/tex} Z aRb if and only if a-b is divisible by n. Show that R is an equivalence relation.
question_html: Let n be a fixed positive integer. Define a relation R in Z as follows {tex}\forall{/tex}a, b {tex}\in{/tex} Z aRb if and only if a-b is divisible by n. Show that R is an equivalence relation.
solution_text: Given that, {tex}\forall{/tex} a, b {tex}\in{/tex} Z aRb if and only a-b is divisible by n Now Reflexive aRa {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (a - a) is divisible by n, which is true for any integer 'a' as ‘0’ is divisible by n. Hence, R is reflexive. Symmetric aRb {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a-b is divisible by n. {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -b + a is divisible by n. {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -(b - a) is divisible by n. {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (b - a) is divisible by n. {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} bRa Hence, R is symmetric. Transitive Let aRb and bRc {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (a-b) is divisible by n and (b-c) is divisible by n {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (a-b) +(b-c) is divisible by n {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (a-c) is divisible by n {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} aRc Hence, R is transitive. So, R is an equivalence relation.
solution_html:
Given that, {tex}\forall{/tex}a, b {tex}\in{/tex} Z aRb if and only a-b is divisible by n
Now

  1. Reflexive
    aRa {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (a - a) is divisible by n, which is true for any integer 'a' as ‘0’ is divisible by n. Hence, R is reflexive.
  2. Symmetric
    aRb
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a-b is divisible by n.
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -b + a is divisible by n.
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -(b - a) is divisible by n.
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (b - a) is divisible by n.
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} bRa
    Hence, R is symmetric.
  3. Transitive
    Let aRb and bRc
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (a-b) is divisible by n and (b-c) is divisible by n
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (a-b) +(b-c) is divisible by n
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (a-c) is divisible by n
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} aRc
    Hence, R is transitive.
    So, R is an equivalence relation.

question_text: If A = {1, 2, 3, 4 }, define relation on A which have properties of being: reflexive, transitive but not symmetric
question_html: If A = {1, 2, 3, 4 }, define relation on A which have properties of being: reflexive, transitive but not symmetric
solution_text: Given that, A = {1, 2, 3, 4 } We have to define a relation which is Reflexive and transitive but not symmetric. Let R be a relation defined by R = {(1,1),(1,2), (1,3),(1,4),(2,2),(2,3),(3,2),(3,3),(4,2), (4,3),(4,4)} on set A. R is reflexive {tex}\because{/tex} (1,1),(2,2),(3,3),(4,4) {tex}\in{/tex} R R is transitive {tex}\because{/tex} (a,b) {tex}\in{/tex} R and (b,c) {tex}\in{/tex} R {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (a,c) {tex}\in{/tex} R for all a,b,c {tex}\in R{/tex} R is not symmetric {tex}\because{/tex} (1,4) {tex}\in{/tex} R but (4,1) {tex}\notin{/tex} R Hence, R is reflexive, transitive but not symmetric.
solution_html:
Given that, A = {1, 2, 3, 4 }
We have to define a relation which is Reflexive and transitive but not symmetric.
Let R be a relation defined by
R = {(1,1),(1,2), (1,3),(1,4),(2,2),(2,3),(3,2),(3,3),(4,2), (4,3),(4,4)} on set A.
R is reflexive {tex}\because{/tex} (1,1),(2,2),(3,3),(4,4) {tex}\in{/tex} R
R is transitive {tex}\because{/tex} (a,b) {tex}\in{/tex} R and (b,c) {tex}\in{/tex} R {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (a,c) {tex}\in{/tex} R for all a,b,c {tex}\in R{/tex}
R is not symmetric {tex}\because{/tex} (1,4) {tex}\in{/tex} R but (4,1) {tex}\notin{/tex} R
Hence, R is reflexive, transitive but not symmetric.

Hence R = {(1, 2), (2, 1)} is symmetric.
Since (1, 1), (2, 2), (3, 3), (4, 4) {tex}\notin{/tex} R, R is not reflexive.
(1, 2) {tex}\in R_{2}{/tex} and (2, 1) {tex}\in R_{2}{/tex} but (1, 1) {tex}\notin{/tex} R2 Hence R2 is not transitive.

question_html:
Let R be relation defined on the set of natural number N as follows:
R = {(x, y): x {tex}\in{/tex} N, y {tex}\in{/tex} N, 2x + y = 41}. Find the domain and range of the relation R. Also verify whether R is reflexive, symmetric and transitive.
solution_html:
Given that,
R = {(1, 39), (2, 37), (3, 35) .... (19, 3), (20, 1)}
Domain = {1,2,3,.......,20}
Range = {1,3,5,7......,39}
R is not reflexive as (2, 2) {tex}\notin{/tex} R as
{tex}2 \times 2 + 2 \ne 41{/tex}
R is not symmetric
as (1, 39) {tex}\in{/tex} R but (39, 1) {tex}\notin{/tex} R
R is not transitive
as (11, 19) {tex}\in{/tex} R, (19, 3) {tex}\in{/tex} R
But (11, 3) {tex}\notin{/tex} R
Hence, R is neither reflexive, nor symmetric and nor transitive.

question_text: Given A = {2, 3, 4}, B = {2, 5, 6, 7}. Construct an example of an injective mapping from A to B
question_html: Given A = {2, 3, 4}, B = {2, 5, 6, 7}. Construct an example of an injective mapping from A to B
solution_text: Given that, A = {2, 3, 4}, B = {2, 5, 6, 7} an injective mapping from A to B Let f : A {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} B denote a mapping f = {(x,y) : y = x + 3} Now, y = x + 3 When x = 2 we get y = 5 When x = 3 we get y = 6 and 8 and When x = 4, we get y = 7 {tex}\therefore{/tex} f = {(2,5),(3,6),(4,7)} We observe that each element of A has unique image in B. Thus, f is injective.
solution_html:
Given that, A = {2, 3, 4}, B = {2, 5, 6, 7}
an injective mapping from A to B
Let f : A {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} B denote a mapping f = {(x,y) : y = x + 3}
Now, y = x + 3
When x = 2 we get y = 5
When x = 3 we get y = 6 and 8
and When x = 4, we get y = 7
{tex}\therefore{/tex} f = {(2,5),(3,6),(4,7)}
We observe that each element of A has unique image in B.
Thus, f is injective.

  3. which is neither one-one nor onto.
solution_text: Let {tex}f:N \to N{/tex} , be a mapping defined by f (x) = 2x Which is one-one For f(x 1 ) = f(x 2 ) {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} 2x 1 = 2x 2 x 1 = x 2 Further f is not onto, as for 1 {tex}\in{/tex} N, there does not exist any x in N such that f(x) = 1. Let {tex}f:N \to N{/tex} given by f(1) = f(2) = 1 and f (x) = x - 1 for every x > 2 is onto but not one-one. f is not one-one as f (1) = f (2) = 1. But f is onto. The mapping {tex}f:R \to R{/tex} defined as f (x) = x 2 , is neither one-one not onto.

    Which is one-one

question_text: Let A = R - {3}, B = R - {1]. If {tex}f:A \to B{/tex} be defined by {tex}f(x) = \frac{{x - 2}}{{x - 3}}\;\forall x \in A{/tex} . Then, show that {tex}f{/tex} is bijective.
question_html: Let A = R - {3}, B = R - {1]. If {tex}f:A \to B{/tex} be defined by {tex}f(x) = \frac{{x - 2}}{{x - 3}}\;\forall x \in A{/tex}. Then, show that {tex}f{/tex} is bijective.
solution_text: Given that, A = R - {3}, B = R - {1}. {tex}f:A \to B{/tex} is defined by {tex}f(x) = \frac{{x - 2}}{{x - 3}}\;\forall x \in A{/tex} For injectivity Let {tex}f({x_1}) = f({x_2}) \Rightarrow \frac{{{x_1} - 2}}{{{x_1} - 3}} = \frac{{{x_2} - 2}}{{{x_2} - 3}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x 1 - 2)(x 2 - 3) = (x 2 - 2)(x 1 - 3) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x 1 x 2 - 3x 1 - 2x 2 + 6 = x 1 x 2 - 3x 2 - 2x 1 + 6 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -3x 1 - 2x 2 = -3x 2 - 2x 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -x 1 = -x 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x 1 = x 2 So, {tex}f{/tex} (x) is an injective function For surjectivity Let {tex}y = \frac{{x - 2}}{{x - 3}} \Rightarrow{/tex} x - 2 = xy - 3y {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x(1 - y) = 2 - 3y {tex} \Rightarrow x = \frac{{2 - 3y}}{{1 - y}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x = \frac{{3y - 2}}{{y - 1}} \in A,\;\forall y \in B{/tex} [codomain] So, {tex}f{/tex} (x) is surjective function. Hence, {tex}f{/tex} (x) is a bijective function.
solution_html:
Given that, A = R - {3}, B = R - {1}.
{tex}f:A \to B{/tex} is defined by {tex}f(x) = \frac{{x - 2}}{{x - 3}}\;\forall x \in A{/tex}
For injectivity
Let {tex}f({x_1}) = f({x_2}) \Rightarrow \frac{{{x_1} - 2}}{{{x_1} - 3}} = \frac{{{x_2} - 2}}{{{x_2} - 3}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x1 - 2)(x2 - 3) = (x2 - 2)(x1 - 3)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x1x2 - 3x1 - 2x2 + 6 = x1x2 - 3x2 - 2x1 + 6
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -3x1 - 2x2 = -3x2 - 2x1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -x1 = -x2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x1 = x2
So, {tex}f{/tex}(x) is an injective function
For surjectivity
Let {tex}y = \frac{{x - 2}}{{x - 3}} \Rightarrow{/tex} x - 2 = xy - 3y
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x(1 - y) = 2 - 3y {tex} \Rightarrow x = \frac{{2 - 3y}}{{1 - y}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow x = \frac{{3y - 2}}{{y - 1}} \in A,\;\forall y \in B{/tex} [codomain]
So, {tex}f{/tex}(x) is surjective function.
Hence, {tex}f{/tex}(x) is a bijective function.

Let A = [–1, 1]. Then, discuss whether the function defined on A by:  f(x) = {tex}\frac{x}{2}{/tex}
is one-one, onto or bijective.
solution_text: Given that, A = [–1, 1] f(x) = {tex}\frac{x}{2}{/tex} Let f(x 1 ) = f(x 2 ) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{x_{1}}{2}=\frac{x_{2}}{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x 1 = 2x 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x 1 = x 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f is one one Now, let f(x) = y {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}y=\frac x2{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}2y= x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} for {tex} y =1 ∈ A{/tex} , we will have x = 2 {tex}\notin{/tex} A {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f is not onto Thus, f is one-one but not onto.
solution_html:
Given that, A = [–1, 1]
f(x) = {tex}\frac{x}{2}{/tex}
Let f(x1) = f(x2)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{x_{1}}{2}=\frac{x_{2}}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x1 = 2x2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x1 = x2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f is one one
Now, let f(x) = y
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}y=\frac x2{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}2y= x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} for {tex} y =1 ∈ A{/tex}, we will have x = 2 {tex}\notin{/tex} A
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f is not onto
Thus, f is one-one but not onto.

question_text: Let A = [–1, 1]. Then, discuss whether the function defined on A by: g(x) = |x| is one-one, onto or bijective.
question_html:
Let A = [–1, 1]. Then, discuss whether the function defined on A by: g(x) = |x|
is one-one, onto or bijective.
solution_text: Given that, A = [–1, 1] let g(x 1 ) = g(x 2 ) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} |x 1 |= |x 2 | {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x 1 = {tex}\pm{/tex} x 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x 1 = x 2 and x 1 = - x 2 ... {e.g., g(-1) = |-1| = 1 and g(1) = |1| = 1} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} g is not one-one. We observe that (-1) does not have any pre-image in the domain since g(x) = |x| assumes only non-negative values. i.e. we cannot find any number in domain which will give (-1) in co-domain. {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} g is not onto Hence, g is neither one one nor onto.
solution_html:
Given that, A = [–1, 1]
let g(x1) = g(x2)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} |x1|= |x2|
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x1 = {tex}\pm{/tex} x2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x1= x2 and x1= - x2   ...{e.g., g(-1) = |-1| = 1 and g(1) = |1| = 1}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} g is not one-one.
We observe that (-1) does not have any pre-image in the domain since g(x) = |x| assumes only non-negative values.
i.e. we cannot find any number in domain which will give (-1) in co-domain.
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} g is not onto
Hence, g is neither one one nor onto.

question_html: Let A = [–1, 1]. Then, discuss whether the function h (x) = x |x|, defined on A is one-one, onto or bijective.
solution_text: Given that, A = [–1, 1] let h(x 1 ) = h(x 2 ) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x 1 |x 1 | = x 2 |x 2 | if x 1, x 2 > 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x 1 2 = x 2 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x 1 = x 2 if x 1, x 2 < 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x 1 2 = x 2 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x 1 = x 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} h is one-one. Let h(x) = y {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = x |x| {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = x 2 Thus, for each y in co-domain there exists x in domain. {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} h is onto. Hence, h is one one and onto. So, h is bijective.
solution_html:
Given that, A = [–1, 1]
let h(x1) = h(x2)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x1|x1| = x2|x2|
if x1,x2 > 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x12 = x22
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x1 = x2
if x1,x2 < 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x12 = x22
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x1 = x2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} h is one-one.
Let h(x) = y
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = x |x|
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = x2
Thus, for each y in co-domain there exists x in domain.
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} h is onto.
Hence, h is one one and onto.
So, h is bijective.

question_text: Define a relation on N by: R = {(x, y)| x is greater than y; {tex}x, y \in N {/tex} } Determine whether the relation R is reflexive, symmetric and transitive.
question_html:
Define a relation on N by: 
  R = {(x, y)| x is greater than y;  {tex}x, y \in N {/tex}}
Determine whether the relation R is reflexive, symmetric and transitive.
solution_text: Given relation is: R = {(x,y): x is greater than y {tex}\forall{/tex} x,y {tex}\in{/tex} N} be a relation defined on N. Now, We observe that any element x {tex}\in{/tex} N cannot be greater than itself. {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x,x) {tex}\notin{/tex} R {tex}\forall{/tex} x {tex}\in{/tex} N {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} R is not reflexive. Let (x,y) {tex}\in{/tex} R {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x is greater than y But y cannot be greater than x if x is greater than y. {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (y,x) {tex}\notin{/tex} R {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} R is not symmetric Let (x,y) {tex}\in{/tex} R and (y,z) {tex}\in{/tex} R {tex}\forall{/tex} x, y,z {tex}\in{/tex} N {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x > y and y > z {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x > z {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x,z) {tex}\in{/tex} R {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} R is transitive. Thus, R is transitive but not reflexive and not symmetric.
solution_html:
Given relation is:
 R = {(x,y): x is greater than y {tex}\forall{/tex} x,y {tex}\in{/tex} N} be a relation defined on N.
Now,
We observe that any element x {tex}\in{/tex} N cannot be greater than itself.

{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (y,x) {tex}\notin{/tex} R

Let (x,y) {tex}\in{/tex} R and (y,z) {tex}\in{/tex} R {tex}\forall{/tex} x, y,z {tex}\in{/tex} N

question_text: Using the definition, prove that the function {tex}f:A \to B{/tex} is invertible if and only if f is both one-one and onto.
question_html: Using the definition, prove that the function {tex}f:A \to B{/tex} is invertible if and only if f is both one-one and onto.
solution_text: Let f:A {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} B be invertible,then there exists a function g:B {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} A such that gof = {tex}I_A{/tex} and fog = {tex}I_B{/tex} . f is one-one:Let x,y {tex}\in{/tex} A such that f(x) = f(y).Then, f(x)=f(y) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} g(f(x))=g(f(y)) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} gof(x)=gof(y) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}I_A(x)=I_B(y){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = y {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f is one-one. f is onto:Let y {tex}\in {/tex} B and let g(y)=x.Then, g(y)=x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f(g(y))=f(x) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} fog(y)=f(x) {tex}I_B(y)=f(x) {/tex} y=f(x) Thus,for each y in B,there exists x in A such that f(x)=y.So,f is onto. Conversely,if f is one-one, and onto ,then f is invertible.
solution_html:
Let f:A {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} B be invertible,then there exists a function g:B {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} A such that gof = {tex}I_A{/tex} and fog = {tex}I_B{/tex}.
f is one-one:Let x,y {tex}\in{/tex} A such that f(x) = f(y).Then,
f(x)=f(y)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} g(f(x))=g(f(y))
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} gof(x)=gof(y)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}I_A(x)=I_B(y){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = y
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f is one-one.
f is onto:Let y{tex}\in {/tex}B and let g(y)=x.Then,
g(y)=x
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f(g(y))=f(x)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} fog(y)=f(x)
{tex}I_B(y)=f(x) {/tex}
y=f(x)
Thus,for each y in B,there exists x in A such that f(x)=y.So,f is onto.
Conversely,if f is one-one, and onto ,then f is invertible.

solution_html:

{tex}= (4x^2 - 12x + 9) + 6x – 9 +1{/tex}

question_text: Let {tex} *{/tex} be the binary operation defined on Q. Find whether the binary operation is commutative: where {tex}a * b = a – b{/tex} {tex}\forall{/tex} {tex}a, b{/tex} {tex}\in{/tex} Q
question_html:

where  {tex}a * b = a – b{/tex} {tex}\forall{/tex} {tex}a, b{/tex} {tex}\in{/tex} Q
solution_text: Given that, {tex}*{/tex} is a binary operation defined on Q. A binary operation {tex} ‘*’ {/tex} is commutative if {tex}a*b = b*a,~~\forall~ a, b \in Q{/tex} {tex}a * b = a – b,~~~\forall~~ a, b \in Q{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}a * b = a – b = -b+a = - (b-a) = - (b*a){/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}a*b \neq b*a{/tex} Hence, {tex} *{/tex} is not commutative on Q.
solution_html:
Given that, {tex}*{/tex}  is a binary operation defined on Q.
A binary operation  {tex} ‘*’ {/tex} is commutative if {tex}a*b = b*a,~~\forall~ a, b \in Q{/tex}   
{tex}a * b = a – b,~~~\forall~~  a, b \in Q{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}a * b = a – b = -b+a = - (b-a) = - (b*a){/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex}  {tex}a*b \neq b*a{/tex}
Hence,{tex} *{/tex} is not commutative on Q.

A binary operation ‘*’ is commutative if a*b = b*a {tex}\forall{/tex} a, b {tex}\in{/tex} Q Now, a * b = (a – b) 2 {tex}\forall{/tex} a, b {tex}\in{/tex} Q {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a * b = (a – b) 2 = {-(b-a)} 2 = (b-a) 2 = b*a {tex}\therefore{/tex} a*b = b*a Hence, ‘*’ is commutative on Q.

solution_text: {tex}\frac{x}{\sqrt{1+3 x^{2}}}{/tex} Given that, f(x) = {tex}\frac{x}{\sqrt{1+x^{2}}}{/tex} Now, (f o f o f)(x) = f[f(f(x))] = {tex}\mathrm{f}\left[\mathrm{f}\left(\frac{\mathrm{x}}{\sqrt{1+\mathrm{x}^{2}}}\right)\right]{/tex} = {tex}\mathrm{f}\left[\frac{\frac{\mathrm{x}}{\sqrt{1+\mathrm{x}^{2}}}}{\sqrt{1+\frac{\mathrm{x}^{2}}{1+\mathrm{x}^{2}}}}\right]{/tex} = {tex}f\left[\frac{\frac{x}{\sqrt{1+x^{2}}}}{\sqrt{\frac{1+x^{2}+x^{2}}{1+x^{2}}}}\right]{/tex} = {tex}f\left[\frac{\frac{x}{\sqrt{1+x^{2}}}}{\sqrt{\frac{1+2 x^{2}}{1+x^{2}}}}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\mathrm{f}\left[\frac{\mathrm{x}}{\sqrt{1+2 \mathrm{x}^{2}}}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\frac{x}{\sqrt{1+2 x^{2}}}}{\sqrt{1+\frac{x^{2}}{1+2 x^{2}}}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\frac{x}{\sqrt{1+2 x^{2}}}}{\sqrt{\frac{1+2 x^{2}+x^{2}}{1+2 x^{2}}}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{x}{\sqrt{1+3 x^{2}}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} (f o f o f) (x) = {tex}\frac{x}{\sqrt{1+3 x^{2}}}{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}\frac{x}{\sqrt{1+3 x^{2}}}{/tex}
Given that, f(x) = {tex}\frac{x}{\sqrt{1+x^{2}}}{/tex}
Now, (f o f o f)(x) = f[f(f(x))]
= {tex}\mathrm{f}\left[\mathrm{f}\left(\frac{\mathrm{x}}{\sqrt{1+\mathrm{x}^{2}}}\right)\right]{/tex}
={tex}\mathrm{f}\left[\frac{\frac{\mathrm{x}}{\sqrt{1+\mathrm{x}^{2}}}}{\sqrt{1+\frac{\mathrm{x}^{2}}{1+\mathrm{x}^{2}}}}\right]{/tex}
={tex}f\left[\frac{\frac{x}{\sqrt{1+x^{2}}}}{\sqrt{\frac{1+x^{2}+x^{2}}{1+x^{2}}}}\right]{/tex}
={tex}f\left[\frac{\frac{x}{\sqrt{1+x^{2}}}}{\sqrt{\frac{1+2 x^{2}}{1+x^{2}}}}\right]{/tex}
= {tex}\mathrm{f}\left[\frac{\mathrm{x}}{\sqrt{1+2 \mathrm{x}^{2}}}\right]{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{\frac{x}{\sqrt{1+2 x^{2}}}}{\sqrt{1+\frac{x^{2}}{1+2 x^{2}}}}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{\frac{x}{\sqrt{1+2 x^{2}}}}{\sqrt{\frac{1+2 x^{2}+x^{2}}{1+2 x^{2}}}}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{x}{\sqrt{1+3 x^{2}}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} (f o f o f) (x) = {tex}\frac{x}{\sqrt{1+3 x^{2}}}{/tex}

solution_text: Answer = 7 + (4 - x) {tex}\frac{1}{3}{/tex} Given that, f (x) = {4 – (x–7) 3 } Let f(x) = y, {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = {4 – (x–7) 3 } {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y – 4 = – (x–7) 3 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 4 – y = (x–7) 3 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x–7) 3 = 4 – y {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x - 7 = (4 - y) {tex}\frac{1}{3}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 7 + (4 - y) {tex}\frac{1}{3}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f -1 (y) = 7 + (4 - y) {tex}\frac{1}{3}{/tex} [ {tex}\because{/tex} f(x) = y, {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = f -1 (y)] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f -1 (x) = 7 + (4 - x) {tex}\frac{1}{3}{/tex}
solution_html:
Answer = 7 + (4 - x){tex}\frac{1}{3}{/tex}
Given that, f (x) = {4 – (x–7)3}
Let f(x) = y,
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = {4 – (x–7)3}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y – 4 = – (x–7)3
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 4 – y = (x–7)3
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x–7)3 = 4 – y
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x - 7 = (4 - y){tex}\frac{1}{3}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 7 + (4 - y){tex}\frac{1}{3}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f-1 (y) = 7 + (4 - y){tex}\frac{1}{3}{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} f(x) = y, {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = f-1(y)]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f-1 (x) = 7 + (4 - x){tex}\frac{1}{3}{/tex}

options.explanation: False In order to prove the composition of functions is commutative, we need to show (f {tex}o{/tex} g)(x) = (g {tex}o{/tex} f)(x) Let us suppose, f(x) = 2x, g(x) = 1 + x 2 Now, (f {tex}o{/tex} g)(x) = f(g(x)) = f(1+x 2 ) = 2(1 + x 2 ) = 2 + 2x 2 (i) (g {tex}o{/tex} f)(x) = g(f(x)) = g(2x) = 1 + (2x) 2 = 1 + 4x 2 (ii) From (i) and (ii), we observe that (f {tex}o{/tex} g)(x) {tex}\neq{/tex} (g {tex}o{/tex} f)(x) Thus, composition of functions is not commutative.

question_html:
Let * be a binary operation defined on Q. Find whether the binary operation is associative:
where a * b = a – b for a, b {tex}\in{/tex} Q.
solution_text: * is not associative for if we take a = 1, b = 2 and c = 3, then (a * b) * c = (1 * 2) * 3 = (1 – 2) * 3 = – 1 – 3 = – 4 and a * (b * c) = 1 * (2 * 3) = 1 * (2 – 3) = 1 – ( – 1) = 2. Thus, (a * b) * c {tex}\neq{/tex} a * (b * c) and hence * is not associative.

Thus,  (a * b) * c {tex}\neq{/tex} a * (b * c) and hence * is not associative.

question_html:
Let * be a binary operation defined on Q. Find whether the binary operation is associative 
where, 
a * b = a – b + ab,   {tex}\forall{/tex}  a, b {tex}\in{/tex} Q.
solution_text: Clearly, for a = 2, b = 3 and c = 4, we have {tex}(a * b) * c = (2 * 3) * 4 = (2 – 3 + 6) * 4 = 5 * 4 = 5 – 4 + 20 = 21 {/tex} {tex}a*(b*c)=2*(3*4)=2*(3-4+12)=2*11=2-11+22=13{/tex} Therefore, {tex}(a*b)*c\neq a*(b*c){/tex} , i.e, operation is not associative.
solution_html:

{tex}a*(b*c)=2*(3*4)=2*(3-4+12)=2*11=2-11+22=13{/tex}
Therefore, {tex}(a*b)*c\neq a*(b*c){/tex}, i.e, operation is not associative.

question_html: Let * be a binary operation defined on Q. Find whether the binary operation is associative {tex}a * b = ab^2{/tex} {tex}\forall{/tex}{tex} a, b \in Q{/tex}
solution_text: Take a = 1, b = 2 and c = 3, then {tex}(a * b) * c = (1 * 2) * 3 = 4 * 3 = 4 9 = 36{/tex} and {tex}a * (b * c) = 1 * (2 * 3) = 1 * 18 = 1 \times 18^2 = 324{/tex} . Thus {tex}(a * b) * c \neq a * (b * c){/tex} and hence * is not associative.
solution_html:
Take a = 1, b = 2 and c = 3,
then {tex}(a * b) * c = (1 * 2) * 3 = 4 * 3 = 4 9 = 36{/tex}
and {tex}a * (b * c) = 1 * (2 * 3) = 1 * 18 = 1 \times 18^2 = 324{/tex}.
Thus {tex}(a * b) * c \neq a * (b * c){/tex} and hence * is not associative.
